title: Oropharyngeal Airway (OPA)
content_markdown:
#### Indications
- To prevent airway obstruction from the tongue in a patient without a gag reflex.
- Used as a bite block post ETT insertion unless a Thomas block has been used.

#### Procedure

- Check and clear the airway of any obstructions.
**If there is a foreign body in the oropharynx inserting an OPA could force the obstruction further into the airway.**

- Select the right size OPA: 
- Measure from the corner of the patient’s mouth to the ear lobe (see **Figure 1**).

- Position patient’s head and open the mouth.

- Insertion: 
- **Adult:** Insert the OPA halfway so that the tip is facing towards the roof of the mouth (see **Figure 2**) and then turn 180\xb0 over the patient’s tongue and insert until the flange is resting against the patient’s lips (see **Figure 3**).
- **Children 

Figure 1

Figure 2

Figure 3

#### Contraindications
- Gag reflex.

#### Complications
- Gagging, vomiting and aspiration.
- Trauma to soft tissues, tongue, palate and pharynx.
- Improper sizing can either push the tongue into the oropharynx or occlude the epiglottis.
#### Specific Notes
- The OPA can be removed if necessary ie if the patient has or develops a gag reflex.
- An incorrectly sized OPA can become an airway obstruction. If the OPA is not achieving its aim then remove it and use alternate airway management techniques.

 **OPA size**  **Patient size (guide only)** 
 000 - 3cm  Newborn to one month old 
**Please note it is not currently recommend using an OPA for this age group.** 
 00 - 5 cm  One month to six months old 
 0 - 6 cm  Six months to three years old 
 1 - 7 cm  Three years to ten years 
 2 - 8 cm  Adolescent / average adult female 
 3 - 9 cm  Average adult male / large adult female 
 4 - 10 cm  Large adult male
